story_title: The California Gold Rush — How a Carpenter's Discovery Built a State
summary: When James Marshall spotted flakes of gold at John Sutter's sawmill on 24 January 1848, he triggered the largest mass migration in American history: 300,000 prospectors poured into California within six years, transforming a frontier territory into the most populous state in the Union and permanently reshaping the US economy.
recommended_arc: Hook: a carpenter's accidental fleck of yellow at a Sierra Nevada millrace. Rising: word spreads, ships from every continent converge on San Francisco. Climax: $2 billion in gold extracted in six years. Payoff: California achieves statehood; the US dollar gets its gold backbone.

verified_facts.claim: James Marshall discovered gold at Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California on 24 January 1848, while building a water-powered sawmill for landowner John Sutter.
News of the discovery remained largely local until President James K. Polk confirmed it in his address to Congress on 5 December 1848, triggering mass migration to California.
Approximately 300,000 people — known as '49ers — came to California between 1848 and 1855 from across the United States and from countries including Mexico, Chile, China, Australia, and the UK.
San Francisco grew from a village of roughly 1,000 residents in 1848 to a city of about 25,000 by 1850; California's non-Indigenous population ballooned from 14,000 in 1848 to 250,000 by 1855.
Total gold extracted during the California Gold Rush is estimated at approximately $2 billion in 19th-century values (equivalent to more than $60 billion in 2020 dollars).

California was admitted to the Union as the 31st state on 9 September 1850, less than two years after the gold discovery — rapid statehood made possible by the explosive population growth the Rush had produced.

The Gold Rush displaced and decimated California's Indigenous population: an estimated 100,000 Native Californians died between 1848 and 1870 as a result of violence, disease, and displacement driven by the influx of settlers.
